reasoning: The option-adjusted spread for the ICE BofA US Corporate B Index jumped from 3.51 on March 26 to 3.73 on March 27, following a massive equity market selloff and Trump's announcement extending a deadline for Iran to open the Strait of Hormuz to Monday, April 6. The forecast date measures the spread on April 5 (which will capture Friday, April 3's closing value) compared to March 29 (which captures Friday, March 27's closing value of 3.73). Because April 3 is the Friday immediately preceding the April 6 deadline, markets are highly likely to de-risk over the weekend, driving credit spreads even wider than they are now. Even though Trump extended the deadline on Thursday, March 26, spreads still widened by 22 bps the next day, showing that mere extensions do not compress the risk premium. Unless a full peace deal is reached before April 3, the fear of strike/escalation will keep spreads above 3.73. The probability of no deal by Friday afternoon is high given the dynamics of last-minute geopolitical negotiations. Therefore, there is a strong probability (~72%) that spreads will remain elevated or increase further.
